```{python}
    with two independently-highlighted 'active' buttons at once — a dropdown has exactly
    one current selection and stays compact regardless of label length."""
    return [dict(
        type="dropdown",
        buttons=[dict(label=arch, method="update", args=[args_fn(i)])
                 for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL)],
        x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=y, yanchor="top",
        showactive=True,
        bgcolor="#f0f0f0", bordercolor="#cccccc", borderwidth=1,
        font=dict(size=12, color="#222"),
        pad=dict(l=10, r=10, t=4, b=4),
    )]
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Confusion matrix

```{python}
#| label: fig-confusion-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrix on the Amazon test set"

from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

n_archs = len(ARCHS_AVAIL)
fig = go.Figure()
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    d  = preds_by_arch[arch]
    cm = confusion_matrix(d["y_test"], d["y_pred"], normalize="true")
    fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
        z=cm,
        x=STAR_LABELS, y=STAR_LABELS,
        colorscale="Viridis", zmin=0, zmax=1,
        text=[[f"{v:.2f}" for v in row_] for row_ in cm],
        texttemplate="%{text}",
        colorbar=dict(title="Fraction"),
        hovertemplate="Pred=%{x}<br>True=%{y}<br>fraction=%{z:.3f}<extra></extra>",
        visible=(i == 0),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Predicted", yaxis_title="True",
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"),
    height=500, width=540,
    margin=dict(t=125, r=20),
    updatemenus=arch_dropdown(lambda i: {"visible": [j == i for j in range(n_archs)]}),
    annotations=[dict(
        text="<b>Choose the model:</b>", x=0.5, xanchor="center", xref="paper",
        y=1.21, yanchor="bottom", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555"),
    )],
)
fig.show()
```

### Error analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-error-seg-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Prediction distance per architecture"

import random
from collections import Counter
random.seed(42)

SEG_KEYS   = ["Correct", "Off by 1", "Off by 2", "Off by 3+"]
SEG_COLORS = {"Correct": "#43A047", "Off by 1": "#FDD835", "Off by 2": "#FB8C00", "Off by 3+": "#E53935"}
n_archs    = len(ARCHS_AVAIL)
labels_d   = [f"±{dd} star{'s' if dd>1 else ''}" for dd in range(1, 5)]

arch_segs, arch_bias = {}, {}
for arch in ARCHS_AVAIL:
    d        = preds_by_arch[arch]
    y_t, y_p = d["y_test"], d["y_pred"]
    dist     = np.abs(y_p - y_t)
    n        = len(dist)
    arch_segs[arch] = {
        "Correct":   (dist == 0).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 1":  (dist == 1).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 2":  (dist == 2).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 3+": (dist >= 3).sum() / n * 100,
    }
    signed = y_p - y_t
    n_tot  = len(signed)
    arch_bias[arch] = {
        "over":  [(signed ==  dd).sum() / n_tot * 100 for dd in range(1, 5)],
        "under": [(signed == -dd).sum() / n_tot * 100 for dd in range(1, 5)],
    }

fig1 = go.Figure()
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    segs = arch_segs[arch]
    for seg in SEG_KEYS:
        pct = segs[seg]
        fig1.add_trace(go.Bar(
            x=[pct], y=[arch], orientation="h",
            name=seg, legendgroup=seg, showlegend=(i == 0),
            marker_color=SEG_COLORS[seg],
            text=f"<b>{seg}</b><br>{pct:.1f}%",
            textposition="inside", insidetextanchor="middle",
            textfont=dict(size=12, color="white" if seg != "Off by 1" else "#333"),
            hovertemplate=f"<b>{seg}</b>: {pct:.1f}%<extra></extra>",
        ))

fig1.update_layout(
    barmode="relative",
    template="simple_white",
    xaxis=dict(range=[0, 100], ticksuffix="%", showgrid=False, title_text="% of test examples"),
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=-0.22, yanchor="top", itemclick=False, itemdoubleclick=False),
    height=360, margin=dict(t=20, b=80),
)
fig1.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-error-bias-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Directional bias per architecture"

fig2 = go.Figure()
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    b = arch_bias[arch]
    fig2.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=[-u for u in b["under"]], y=labels_d,
        orientation="h", name="Under-predicted",
        legendgroup="under", showlegend=False,
        marker_color="#1565C0",
        text=[f"{u:.1f}%" for u in b["under"]],
        textposition="outside", textfont=dict(size=11, color="#333"), cliponaxis=False,
        hovertemplate="%{y} — under: <b>%{customdata:.1f}%</b><extra></extra>",
        customdata=b["under"],
        visible=(i == 0),
    ))
    fig2.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=b["over"], y=labels_d,
        orientation="h", name="Over-predicted",
        legendgroup="over", showlegend=False,
        marker_color="#E53935",
        text=[f"{v:.1f}%" for v in b["over"]],
        textposition="outside", textfont=dict(size=11, color="#333"), cliponaxis=False,
        hovertemplate="%{y} — over: <b>%{x:.1f}%</b><extra></extra>",
        visible=(i == 0),
    ))

# Traces fantômes permanents pour la légende (toujours visibles)
fig2.add_trace(go.Bar(x=[None], y=[None], orientation="h",
    name="Under-predicted", marker_color="#1565C0",
    legendgroup="under", showlegend=True))
fig2.add_trace(go.Bar(x=[None], y=[None], orientation="h",
    name="Over-predicted", marker_color="#E53935",
    legendgroup="over", showlegend=True))

def make_vis2(k):
    v = []
    for i in range(n_archs):
        v.extend([i == k, i == k])
    v.extend([True, True])  # traces fantômes toujours visibles
    return v

fig2.update_xaxes(
    tickvals=[-20, -15, -10, -5, 0, 5, 10, 15, 20],
    ticktext=["20%", "15%", "10%", "5%", "0", "5%", "10%", "15%", "20%"],
    range=[-22, 22],
    title_text="% of test examples",
)
fig2.update_yaxes(autorange="reversed")
fig2.add_vline(x=0, line_width=1.5, line_color="#333")

fig2.update_layout(
    barmode="relative",
    template="simple_white",
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=-0.18, yanchor="top", itemclick=False, itemdoubleclick=False),
    height=480, margin=dict(t=90, b=80),
    annotations=[dict(
        text="<b>Choose the model:</b>", x=0.5, xanchor="center", xref="paper",
        y=1.22, yanchor="bottom", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555"),
    )],
    updatemenus=arch_dropdown(lambda i: {"visible": make_vis2(i)}),
)
fig2.show()
```

### Model agreement

Rather than asking *which* model is wrong, this view asks a deeper question: when the six architectures disagree, is that because some of them are simply weaker or because the example itself is ambiguous (e.g. a "mixed" review that a human would also struggle to rate)? For each test example, we count how many distinct star ratings the six models collectively predict, then look at (a) how often the **majority vote** is actually correct at each agreement level, and (b) which true ratings dominate the disagreement zone.

```{python}
#| label: fig-agreement-correctness
#| fig-cap: "Model agreement vs. correctness — does disagreement signal a weak model or an inherently ambiguous example?"

preds_matrix = np.array([preds_by_arch[a]["y_pred"] for a in ARCHS_AVAIL])
y_true       = preds_by_arch[ARCHS_AVAIL[0]]["y_test"]
n_test       = preds_matrix.shape[1]

n_distinct  = np.array([len(set(preds_matrix[:, i])) for i in range(n_test)])
maj_pred    = np.array([np.bincount(preds_matrix[:, i], minlength=5).argmax() for i in range(n_test)])
maj_correct = (maj_pred == y_true)

levels  = sorted(np.unique(n_distinct).tolist())
xlabels = ["All 6 agree" if k == 1 else f"{k} distinct labels" for k in levels]

share = [(n_distinct == k).mean() * 100 for k in levels]
acc   = [maj_correct[n_distinct == k].mean() * 100 for k in levels]

STAR_COLORS = ["#E53935", "#FB8C00", "#FDD835", "#7CB342", "#43A047"]
comp = np.zeros((len(levels), 5))
for li, k in enumerate(levels):
    counts = np.bincount(y_true[n_distinct == k], minlength=5)
    comp[li] = counts / counts.sum() * 100

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=["Share of test set & majority-vote accuracy", "Composition of true ratings"],
)
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=xlabels, y=share, name="% of test set", marker_color="#90A4AE",
    hovertemplate="%{x}<br>%{y:.1f}% of test set<extra></extra>",
), row=1, col=1)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=xlabels, y=acc, name="Majority-vote accuracy", mode="lines+markers",
    marker=dict(size=10, color="#C62828"), line=dict(width=3, color="#C62828"),
    hovertemplate="%{x}<br>accuracy: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>",
), row=1, col=1)

for ci, star in enumerate(STAR_LABELS):
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=xlabels, y=comp[:, ci], name=star, marker_color=STAR_COLORS[ci],
        hovertemplate=f"<b>{star}</b><br>%{{x}}: %{{y:.1f}}% of group<extra></extra>",
    ), row=1, col=2)

fig.update_yaxes(title_text="%", range=[0, 100], row=1, col=1)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="% of group", range=[0, 100], row=1, col=2)
fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-15)
fig.update_layout(
    barmode="stack", template="simple_white", height=440,
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=-0.32, yanchor="top"),
    margin=dict(t=60, b=120),
)
fig.show()
```

**Reading the rendered chart**: the six architectures agree unanimously on 53% of the test set, and there the majority vote is right 76.7% of the time. Agreement collapses quickly past that point, once predictions split into 2 distinct labels (37% of examples), majority-vote accuracy drops to ~48%, then flattens around 30–40% for 3+ distinct labels. The right panel explains why: the share of extreme ratings (1★ and 5★ over half of the "all agree" group) shrinks steadily as disagreement grows, replaced by middle ratings (2★–4★), exactly the boundary cases where a human annotator would also hesitate between adjacent stars. At the extreme (5 distinct predictions, well under 1% of the test set : too few examples to read anything into the small accuracy uptick there), the composition is almost purely 2★/3★: a textbook "mildly negative or neutral?" ambiguity. This backs the **label-ambiguity** reading — a meaningful share of the remaining error sits in inherently fuzzy middle-rating reviews, a ceiling that no architecture change is likely to break — while the ~23% error rate that persists even where all six models agree points to a second, smaller pool of genuinely hard or mislabeled examples shared across architectures.

### Examples

```{python}
#| label: fig-examples-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Example predictions on the Amazon test set"

import json, random
from IPython.display import HTML

random.seed(42)

y_test = preds_by_arch[ARCHS_AVAIL[0]]["y_test"]
X_test = preds_by_arch[ARCHS_AVAIL[0]]["X_test"]
texts  = X_test.tolist() if X_test.ndim == 1 else X_test[:, 0].tolist()

df_ex = pd.DataFrame({"true": y_test, "text": texts})
for arch in ARCHS_AVAIL:
    df_ex[arch] = preds_by_arch[arch]["y_pred"]

df_ex["disagree"] = df_ex[ARCHS_AVAIL].nunique(axis=1) > 1
df_ex["any_wrong"] = (df_ex[ARCHS_AVAIL].values != df_ex[["true"]].values).any(axis=1)
pool   = df_ex[df_ex["disagree"] & df_ex["any_wrong"]]
sample = pool.sample(12, random_state=42).reset_index(drop=True)

ARCH_SHORT = {"FastText": "FastText", "FastText + Label Attention": "FT+LA",
              "FATE (L=1)": "FATE (L=1)", "FATE (L=1) + Label Attention": "FATE (L=1)+LA",
              "FATE (L=2)": "FATE (L=2)", "FATE (L=2) + Label Attention": "FATE (L=2)+LA"}

examples = [
    {"true": int(r["true"]), "text": str(r["text"]),
     "preds": {a: int(r[a]) for a in ARCHS_AVAIL}}
    for _, r in sample.iterrows()
]

html = f"""
<style>
  .ex-wrap  {{ font-family:sans-serif; font-size:14px; max-width:100%; }}
  .ex-card  {{ border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 24px;
               background:#fafafa; margin-bottom:14px; }}
  .ex-meta  {{ font-size:13px; color:#555; margin-bottom:10px; }}
  .ex-meta b {{ color:#222; }}
  .ex-text  {{ line-height:1.75; color:#222; margin-bottom:18px; }}
  .ex-preds {{ display:flex; gap:12px; flex-wrap:wrap; }}
  .ex-model {{ display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; gap:4px; }}
  .ex-mname {{ font-size:11px; color:#888; font-weight:600; text-transform:uppercase;
               letter-spacing:.04em; }}
  .ex-badge {{ padding:5px 14px; border-radius:5px; font-weight:700; font-size:15px; }}
  .correct  {{ background:#d4edda; color:#155724; }}
  .wrong    {{ background:#f8d7da; color:#721c24; }}
  .ex-nav   {{ display:flex; align-items:center; gap:12px; }}
  .ex-btn   {{ padding:6px 18px; background:#f0f0f0; border:1px solid #ccc;
               border-radius:5px; cursor:pointer; font-size:13px; }}
  .ex-btn:hover {{ background:#e0e0e0; }}
  .ex-count {{ font-size:12px; color:#999; }}
</style>

<div class="ex-wrap">
  <div class="ex-nav" style="justify-content:center; margin-bottom:12px;">
    <button class="ex-btn" onclick="exNav(-1)">← Prev</button>
    <span class="ex-count" id="ex-count"></span>
    <button class="ex-btn" onclick="exNav(1)">Next →</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ex-card">
    <div class="ex-meta" id="ex-meta"></div>
    <div class="ex-text" id="ex-text"></div>
    <div class="ex-preds" id="ex-preds"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {{
  const EX   = {json.dumps(examples)};
  const SL   = {json.dumps(STAR_LABELS)};
  const AS   = {json.dumps(ARCH_SHORT)};
  let cur = 0;

  function render(i) {{
    const e = EX[i];
    document.getElementById('ex-meta').innerHTML =
      'True rating: <b>' + SL[e.true] + '</b>';
    document.getElementById('ex-text').textContent = e.text;
    let h = '';
    for (const [arch, pred] of Object.entries(e.preds)) {{
      const cls = pred === e.true ? 'correct' : 'wrong';
      h += '<div class="ex-model"><span class="ex-mname">' + (AS[arch]||arch) + '</span>'
         + '<span class="ex-badge ' + cls + '">' + SL[pred] + '</span></div>';
    }}
    document.getElementById('ex-preds').innerHTML = h;
    document.getElementById('ex-count').textContent = (i+1) + ' / ' + EX.length;
  }}

  window.exNav = function(d) {{ cur = (cur + d + EX.length) % EX.length; render(cur); }};
  render(0);
}})();
</script>
"""
HTML(html)
```

:::

## IMDb

*Coming soon.*

:::


## Explainability

This section turns the spotlight on *why* the models predict what they predict, not just *how often* they're right. The analysis is built around a focused comparison: our **best-performing model** : FATE (L=2) with Label Attention is set against its direct counterpart **FATE (L=2) without label attention** (identical architecture and hyperparameters, mean pooling as the sole difference). This pairing isolates the contribution of the label attention mechanism to how the model reasons about text. Both models are probed with an identical battery of analyses on the *same* 200 sampled test reviews, so every figure below is a direct, paired comparison rather than two separate reports glued together.

Five complementary instruments are used throughout:

- **Layer Integrated Gradients (Captum)** : a gradient-based, post-hoc attribution method that assigns every input word a *signed* score for every class: positive = pushes the prediction toward that star rating, negative = pushes away. It only needs a forward+backward pass through the model, so it can be computed identically for both architectures which is exactly what makes the comparison fair.
- **Label-attention weights** : for the Label Attention model only: the *raw cross-attention weights* between each class query and the sentence's tokens. An explanation that's *built into* the architecture, rather than reconstructed after the fact.
- **Self-attention matrices** : both models are built on the same transformer backbone, so we can also peek at how the encoder itself distributes attention across the sentence, layer by layer, before any classification happens.
- **Class direction vectors** : the per-class reference vectors the final classification step actually compares the sentence representation to: the label-attention queries (`label_embeds`) for one model, the rows of the linear classification head (`linear_weight`) for the other. Different mechanisms, same role. Comparing their geometry tells us how each architecture *organises* the five star ratings internally.
- **A faithfulness (word-deletion) stress test** : attribution scores are only useful if they reflect what the model *actually* relies on, so we test this directly: delete the highest-scoring words and watch the predicted probability collapse (or not).

Throughout, **blue tones denote evidence *for* a class and red tones evidence *against* it**, and the same five-colour palette (dark blue → green → amber → purple → dark red) consistently encodes 1★ through 5★ in every figure that breaks results down by star rating. Each subsection below states the analysis' goal, shows the figure, and closes with concrete pointers on how to read it.

```{python}
#| label: load-explain-artifacts

import os
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLE_ARTIFACTS_PROGRESS_BAR"] = "false"

import mlflow
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

EXPLAIN_RUN_LABATT  = "8cb23a624c674c80a5f1fe0274706e8e"
EXPLAIN_RUN_POOLING = "2e44179b11ef413eb144984c4e0d9b03"

_ARTIFACT_NAMES = ["captum.npz", "label_attn.npz", "class_vectors.npz", "self_attn.npz", "faithfulness.npz"]

def _load_artifact(run_id: str, name: str):
    """Download (and cache) one explainability artifact for run_id; None if unavailable."""
    if not run_id or run_id.startswith("?"):
        return None
    cache_dir = Path(f"benchmark/results/explain_{run_id[:8]}")
    cache_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cache = cache_dir / name
    sentinel = cache_dir / (name + ".missing")
    if sentinel.exists():
        return None
    if cache.exists():
        return np.load(cache, allow_pickle=True)
    try:
        client = mlflow.MlflowClient()
        with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:
            local = client.download_artifacts(run_id, f"explainability/{name}", dst_path=tmp)
            d = np.load(local, allow_pickle=True)
            np.savez(str(cache), **{k: d[k] for k in d.files})
        return np.load(cache, allow_pickle=True)
    except Exception:
        sentinel.touch()
        return None

cap_la, lat_la, cv_la, sa_la, fai_la = (_load_artifact(EXPLAIN_RUN_LABATT,  n) for n in _ARTIFACT_NAMES)
cap_pl, lat_pl, cv_pl, sa_pl, fai_pl = (_load_artifact(EXPLAIN_RUN_POOLING, n) for n in _ARTIFACT_NAMES)

HAS_LA   = cap_la is not None
HAS_PL   = cap_pl is not None
HAS_BOTH = HAS_LA and HAS_PL

if not HAS_LA:
    print("Explainability artifacts not yet available — re-run benchmark/explain.py")
else:
    N_CLS   = cap_la["word_attn"][0].shape[0]
    STARS   = [f"{i + 1}★" for i in range(N_CLS)]
    PALETTE = ["#1565C0", "#2E7D32", "#F57F17", "#6A1B9A", "#B71C1C"]

    MODEL_LABELS = {"labatt": "Label Attention", "pooling": "Mean Pooling"}
    CAPTUM       = {"labatt": cap_la, "pooling": cap_pl}
    LABEL_ATTN   = {"labatt": lat_la, "pooling": lat_pl}
    CLASS_VECS   = {"labatt": cv_la,  "pooling": cv_pl}
    SELF_ATTN    = {"labatt": sa_la,  "pooling": sa_pl}
    FAITHFUL     = {"labatt": fai_la, "pooling": fai_pl}
    MODEL_KEYS   = [k for k in ("labatt", "pooling") if CAPTUM[k] is not None]

    def _class_sorted(cap, i: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """Reorder word_attn[i]'s rows from confidence rank back to actual class index."""
        inv = np.argsort(cap["class_order"][i])
        return cap["word_attn"][i][inv]
```

### Word attributions

Aggregate statistics are useful, but the fastest way to *trust* or distrust an explanation is to look at it on a real sentence. The figure below renders a handful of test reviews as **highlighted text**: each word's background is shaded in proportion to its Captum attribution score for the **selected class** : use the star buttons to switch class. **Green = pushes toward the selected rating, red = pushes away from it**, with colour intensity proportional to `|score| / max|score|` within that review. By default the selected class is the model's predicted one, but you can inspect any class, including those not predicted, to see what evidence the model associates with each star rating. Both architectures see the *same* review, so you can directly compare which words each one leans on.

The selection below covers four informative cases: reviews where the two architectures **disagree** on the predicted rating (the richest source of qualitative insight: *what* did one model see that the other didn't?); a couple where they **agree and are correct** (a sanity check on what a "good" explanation looks like); cases where **both are wrong** (what misleads them?); and cases where **Mean Pooling is correct but Label Attention is not** (when does the simpler architecture do better?).

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-highlight
#| fig-cap: "Highlighted-text view of Captum attributions. Green = pushes toward the selected class, red = pushes away from it. Use the star buttons to switch class."
import json
from IPython.display import HTML, display

if not HAS_LA:
    out = HTML("<p>Artifacts not yet available.</p>")
else:
    y_true_la = cap_la["y_true"].astype(int)
    y_pred_la = cap_la["y_pred"].astype(int)
    rng = np.random.default_rng(11)

    picks: list = []
    if HAS_BOTH:
        y_pred_pl = cap_pl["y_pred"].astype(int)
        disagree  = np.where(y_pred_la != y_pred_pl)[0]
        rng.shuffle(disagree)
        picks += disagree[:3].tolist()
        agree_ok = np.where((y_pred_la == y_pred_pl) & (y_pred_la == y_true_la))[0]
        rng.shuffle(agree_ok)
        picks += [int(i) for i in agree_ok if i not in picks][:2]
        both_wrong = np.where((y_pred_la != y_true_la) & (y_pred_pl != y_true_la))[0]
        rng.shuffle(both_wrong)
        picks += [int(i) for i in both_wrong if i not in picks][:2]
        pl_right_la_wrong = np.where((y_pred_pl == y_true_la) & (y_pred_la != y_true_la))[0]
        rng.shuffle(pl_right_la_wrong)
        picks += [int(i) for i in pl_right_la_wrong if i not in picks][:2]
    else:
        ok = np.where(y_pred_la == y_true_la)[0]
        rng.shuffle(ok)
        picks = ok[:5].tolist()

    examples_explain = []
    for i in picks:
        i = int(i)
        words = [str(w) for w in list(cap_la["words"][i])[:40]]
        entry = {
            "y_true": int(cap_la["y_true"][i]),
            "text":   str(cap_la["texts"][i])[:200],
            "models": [],
        }
        for key in MODEL_KEYS:
            cap  = CAPTUM[key]
            pred = int(cap["y_pred"][i])
            mat  = _class_sorted(cap, i)
            entry["models"].append({
                "name":        MODEL_LABELS[key],
                "pred":        pred,
                "words":       words,
                "class_scores": [mat[c][:len(words)].tolist() for c in range(N_CLS)],
            })
        examples_explain.append(entry)

    hl_html = f"""
<style>
  .hl-wrap  {{ font-family:sans-serif; font-size:14px; }}
  .hl-nav   {{ display:flex; align-items:center; gap:12px; justify-content:center; margin-bottom:12px; }}
  .hl-btn   {{ padding:6px 18px; background:#f0f0f0; border:1px solid #ccc;
               border-radius:5px; cursor:pointer; font-size:13px; }}
  .hl-btn:hover {{ background:#e0e0e0; }}
  .hl-count {{ font-size:12px; color:#999; }}
  .hl-card  {{ border:1px solid #e2e2e2; border-radius:8px; padding:14px 16px; background:#fcfcfc; }}
  .hl-meta  {{ font-size:13px; color:#444; margin-bottom:10px; }}
  .hl-model {{ margin:6px 0 14px 0; }}
  .hl-mlbl  {{ font-size:12.5px; color:#555; margin-bottom:4px; }}
  .hl-words {{ line-height:2.5; }}
  .hl-cls-btn {{ padding:2px 9px; font-size:11px; border-radius:12px; cursor:pointer;
                 border:1.5px solid; margin:0 2px; transition:all .15s; font-weight:600; }}
</style>

<div class="hl-wrap">
  <div class="hl-nav">
    <button class="hl-btn" onclick="hlNav(-1)">← Prev</button>
    <span class="hl-count" id="hl-count"></span>
    <button class="hl-btn" onclick="hlNav(1)">Next →</button>
  </div>
  <div class="hl-card">
    <div class="hl-meta"   id="hl-meta"></div>
    <div id="hl-models"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {{
  const EX = {json.dumps(examples_explain)};
  const ST = {json.dumps(STARS)};
  const PA = {json.dumps(PALETTE)};
  let cur = 0;
  const hlSel = {{}};

  function esc(s) {{
    return String(s).replace(/&/g,'&amp;').replace(/</g,'&lt;').replace(/>/g,'&gt;');
  }}

  function spans(words, scores) {{
    const amax = Math.max.apply(null, scores.map(function(x){{ return Math.abs(x); }})) || 1;
    return words.map(function(w, j) {{
      const s     = j < scores.length ? scores[j] : 0;
      const t     = Math.abs(s) / amax;
      const rgb   = s >= 0 ? '57,255,20' : '255,30,30';
      const alpha = (0.10 + 0.65 * t).toFixed(2);
      return '<span style="background-color:rgba(' + rgb + ',' + alpha + ');'
           + 'padding:1px 3px;margin:0 1px;line-height:2.3;">'
           + esc(w) + '</span>';
    }}).join(' ');
  }}

  function render(i) {{
    const e = EX[i];
    document.getElementById('hl-count').textContent = (i + 1) + ' / ' + EX.length;
    document.getElementById('hl-meta').innerHTML =
      '<b>Review #' + i + '</b> &mdash; true rating: '
      + '<span style="color:#222;font-weight:700;">' + ST[e.y_true] + '</span>'
      + ' &mdash; <i>&ldquo;' + esc(e.text).slice(0, 170) + '&hellip;&rdquo;</i>';
    let h = '';
    for (let k = 0; k < e.models.length; k++) {{
      const m    = e.models[k];
      const ok   = m.pred === e.y_true;
      const bc   = ok ? '#2E7D32' : '#B71C1C';
      const skey = i + '_' + k;
      const selC = hlSel[skey] !== undefined ? hlSel[skey] : m.pred;
      const btns = ST.map(function(star, c) {{
        const active = c === selC;
        const isPred = c === m.pred;
        const col    = isPred ? bc : '#333';
        return '<button class="hl-cls-btn" onclick="hlSelClass(' + i + ',' + k + ',' + c + ')"'
             + ' style="border-color:' + col + ';color:' + (active ? '#fff' : col) + ';background:' + (active ? col : '#fff') + ';">'
             + star + '</button>';
      }}).join('');
      h += '<div class="hl-model">'
        +  '<div class="hl-mlbl"><b>' + esc(m.name) + '</b> &rarr; predicts '
        +  '<span style="color:#222;font-weight:700;">' + ST[m.pred] + '</span> '
        +  '<span style="color:' + bc + ';font-size:11px;">(' + (ok ? '✓ correct' : '✗ incorrect') + ')</span>'
        +  ' &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; <span style="font-size:11px;color:#888;">attributions for:</span> ' + btns
        +  '</div>'
        +  '<div class="hl-words">' + spans(m.words, m.class_scores[selC]) + '</div>'
        +  '</div>';
    }}
    document.getElementById('hl-models').innerHTML = h;
  }}

  window.hlSelClass = function(exIdx, modelIdx, classIdx) {{
    hlSel[exIdx + '_' + modelIdx] = classIdx;
    render(cur);
  }};
  window.hlNav = function(d) {{ cur = (cur + d + EX.length) % EX.length; render(cur); }};
  render(0);
}})();
</script>
"""
    out = HTML(hl_html)

display(out)
```

### Corpus-level word importance

Zooming out from individual reviews: which words does each architecture rely on *in general* to recognise a 1★ vs. a 5★ review? For every one of the 200 sampled reviews, Captum produces a signed attribution for each word and each class; here we average these scores across all occurrences of each word, keeping only words seen at least 5 times, to avoid one-off noise and display the twelve most positively-attributed words per star rating.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-corpus
#| fig-cap: "Top-12 most positively-attributed words per star rating (mean signed Captum IG score over 200 reviews, words seen ≥ 5 times)."

TOP_K = 12

def _corpus_acc(cap) -> list:
    """For each class, map word (lower-cased) -> list of its signed IG scores across all reviews."""
    acc = [defaultdict(list) for _ in range(N_CLS)]
    for i, wds in enumerate(cap["words"]):
        mat = _class_sorted(cap, i)
        for c in range(N_CLS):
            for j, w in enumerate(list(wds)):
                if j < mat.shape[1]:
                    acc[c][w.lower()].append(float(mat[c, j]))
    return acc

def _corpus_top(acc, c: int, k: int = TOP_K, min_count: int = 5):
    means = {w: float(np.mean(v)) for w, v in acc[c].items() if len(v) >= min_count}
    return sorted(means.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:k]

if not HAS_LA:
    print("Artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    accs = {k: _corpus_acc(CAPTUM[k]) for k in MODEL_KEYS}
    n_tr = N_CLS

    # 2-row × 3-col grid; 6th cell is empty (N_CLS = 5)
    ROWS, COLS = 2, 3
    row_col = [(r + 1, c + 1) for r in range(ROWS) for c in range(COLS)][:N_CLS]
    specs = [[{"type": "xy"}] * COLS,
             [{"type": "xy"}, {"type": "xy"}, None]]

    fig_corp = make_subplots(
        rows=ROWS, cols=COLS,
        subplot_titles=STARS,
        specs=specs,
        horizontal_spacing=0.14,
        vertical_spacing=0.20,
    )

    for ki, key in enumerate(MODEL_KEYS):
        for c, (row, col) in zip(range(N_CLS), row_col):
            top    = _corpus_top(accs[key], c)[::-1]
            ws     = [w for w, _ in top]
            ss     = [s for _, s in top]
            colors = [PALETTE[c] if s >= 0 else "#ef5350" for s in ss]
            fig_corp.add_trace(go.Bar(
                x=ss, y=ws, orientation="h",
                marker=dict(color=colors, line=dict(width=0)),
                width=0.55,
                opacity=0.88,
                showlegend=False,
                visible=(ki == 0),
                hovertemplate="<b>%{y}</b><br>Mean IG: %{x:.4f}<extra></extra>",
            ), row=row, col=col)

    # Axis styling per panel
    for c, (row, col) in zip(range(N_CLS), row_col):
        fig_corp.update_xaxes(
            showgrid=True, gridcolor="#f0f0f0", gridwidth=1,
            zeroline=True, zerolinecolor="#aaa", zerolinewidth=1.5,
            tickfont=dict(size=10),
            title_text="Mean IG score",
            title_font=dict(size=10, color="#777"),
            row=row, col=col,
        )
        fig_corp.update_yaxes(
            tickfont=dict(size=11),
            showgrid=False,
            row=row, col=col,
        )

    # Colour subplot titles to match class palette
    for i, ann in enumerate(fig_corp.layout.annotations):
        if i < N_CLS:
            ann.font.size   = 15
            ann.font.color  = PALETTE[i]
            ann.font.family = "sans-serif"

    layout_extra = {}
    if HAS_BOTH:
        vis = {key: [j // n_tr == ki for j in range(n_tr * len(MODEL_KEYS))]
               for ki, key in enumerate(MODEL_KEYS)}
        layout_extra = dict(
            updatemenus=[dict(
                type="buttons", direction="right", active=0,
                buttons=[dict(label=MODEL_LABELS[key], method="restyle",
                              args=[{"visible": vis[key]}])
                         for key in MODEL_KEYS],
                x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=1.05, yanchor="bottom",
                showactive=True,
                bgcolor="#f5f5f5", bordercolor="#ddd", borderwidth=1,
                font=dict(size=12, color="#222"),
                pad=dict(l=8, r=8, t=4, b=4),
            )],
        )

    fig_corp.update_layout(
        height=680,
        margin=dict(t=110 if HAS_BOTH else 60, b=30, l=20, r=20),
        paper_bgcolor="white",
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        **layout_extra,
    )

    # Centre 4★ and 5★ in the second row
    h_sp  = 0.14
    col_w = (1.0 - (COLS - 1) * h_sp) / COLS
    x4    = (1.0 - (2 * col_w + h_sp)) / 2
    x5    = x4 + col_w + h_sp
    fig_corp.update_layout(
        xaxis4=dict(domain=[x4,       x4 + col_w]),
        xaxis5=dict(domain=[x5,       x5 + col_w]),
        yaxis4=dict(domain=fig_corp.layout.yaxis4.domain),
        yaxis5=dict(domain=fig_corp.layout.yaxis5.domain),
    )
    anns = list(fig_corp.layout.annotations)
    anns[3].x = x4 + col_w / 2
    anns[4].x = x5 + col_w / 2
    fig_corp.update_layout(annotations=anns)

    fig_corp.show()
```

### How focused are the explanations?

A model can be "right for the right reasons" in (at least) two different styles: by **concentrating** its evidence on a handful of decisive words, or by **distributing** it thinly across the whole sentence. Neither is inherently better but the two architectures might default to different styles, and that has practical consequences (concentrated explanations are easier for a human reviewer to audit at a glance).

For each review, we take the predicted class's row of |Captum scores|, normalise it into a probability distribution over the sentence's words, and compute its **Shannon entropy**, itself normalised by `log(n_words)` so that sentences of different lengths remain comparable. The result lives in *[0, 1]*:

- **Close to 0** → almost all the "credit" sits on one or two words, a peaked, easy-to-audit explanation.
- **Close to 1** → the explanation spreads its weight near-uniformly across the sentence : every word "matters a little", which in practice often means *no* word clearly stands out.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-entropy
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of the normalised entropy of |Captum IG| scores for the predicted class, one point per review (200 reviews). Lower = concentrated / peaked explanations; higher = diffuse explanations."

def _focus_entropy(cap) -> np.ndarray:
    vals = []
    for i in range(len(cap["words"])):
        nw = len(cap["words"][i])
        if nw < 2:
            continue
        s = np.abs(cap["word_attn"][i][0][:nw])   # row 0 = top-1 predicted class (confidence order)
        tot = s.sum()
        if tot <= 0:
            continue
        p = s / tot
        h = -np.sum(p * np.log(p + 1e-12))
        vals.append(h / np.log(nw))
    return np.array(vals)

if not HAS_LA:
    print("Artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    fig_ent = go.Figure()
    for key in MODEL_KEYS:
        vals  = _focus_entropy(CAPTUM[key])
        color = PALETTE[0] if key == "labatt" else PALETTE[4]
        fig_ent.add_trace(go.Violin(
            y=vals, name=MODEL_LABELS[key],
            line_color=color, fillcolor=color, opacity=0.40,
            box=dict(visible=True, fillcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.85)",
                     line=dict(color=color, width=1.5)),
            meanline=dict(visible=True, color="white", width=3),
            points="all", pointpos=0, jitter=0.35, marker=dict(size=3, opacity=0.35),
        ))
    fig_ent.update_layout(
        yaxis_title="Normalised entropy of |attribution|  (0 = peaked, 1 = diffuse)",
        yaxis_range=[0, 1.02], paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
        height=440, margin=dict(t=30, b=20, l=10, r=10), showlegend=False,
    )
    fig_ent.show()
```

### Are per-class explanations distinctive?

A genuinely useful per-class explanation should look *different* depending on which class it explains, otherwise the model (or Captum) is essentially saying "this word is generically important", which is far less actionable than "this word specifically signals a 5★ review".

To measure this, we compute a 5×5 **Pearson correlation matrix** between the classes' word-attribution profiles. The construction is:

1. Reuse the per-class mean IG scores from the previous section. For each word seen ≥ 5 times in the 200 reviews, we already have its **mean signed IG score for each of the 5 classes** : call this a vocabulary profile per class.
2. This gives 5 vectors of length |vocabulary|, one per star rating, where entry $j$ reads *"how much does word $j$ push toward class $c$ on average?"*
3. `np.corrcoef` computes the Pearson $r$ between every pair of these class vectors, yielding the 5×5 symmetric matrix shown below.

**How to read it:** the diagonal is always 1 by construction. **Off-diagonal cells near +1** mean two classes share the same signed vocabulary — e.g. a strong positive correlation between 4★ and 5★ means the model tells them apart by *degree* rather than by *different words* (plausible: they are semantically adjacent). **Cells near −1** mean mirror-image vocabularies: what pushes strongly toward 1★ pushes equally strongly *away* from 5★. **Cells near 0** mean the two classes draw on largely unrelated evidence.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-class-correlation
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlation between classes' corpus-level word-attribution profiles, per model. Values near +1: two ratings rely on a very similar (signed) vocabulary; near -1: mirror-image vocabularies; near 0: unrelated vocabularies."

def _class_corpus_matrix(acc, min_count: int = 5) -> np.ndarray:
    vocab = sorted({w for c in acc for w, v in c.items() if len(v) >= min_count})
    mat = np.zeros((N_CLS, len(vocab)))
    for c in range(N_CLS):
        for j, w in enumerate(vocab):
            v = acc[c].get(w)
            if v and len(v) >= min_count:
                mat[c, j] = float(np.mean(v))
    return mat

if not HAS_LA:
    print("Artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    fig_corr = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=len(MODEL_KEYS),
                             subplot_titles=[MODEL_LABELS[k] for k in MODEL_KEYS])
    for ci, key in enumerate(MODEL_KEYS):
        corr = np.corrcoef(_class_corpus_matrix(accs[key]))
        fig_corr.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
            z=corr, x=STARS, y=STARS, zmin=-1, zmax=1, colorscale="RdBu_r",
            text=np.round(corr, 2), texttemplate="%{text}", textfont=dict(size=11),
            showscale=(ci == len(MODEL_KEYS) - 1), colorbar=dict(title="r"),
        ), row=1, col=ci + 1)
    fig_corr.update_yaxes(autorange="reversed")
    fig_corr.update_layout(height=380, margin=dict(t=60, b=10, l=10, r=10),
                           paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white")
    fig_corr.show()
```


### Self-attention: how the transformer reads the sentence, layer by layer

Both architectures are built on the **same transformer backbone** : they only differ in how its token-level outputs get turned into a single classification decision. So before looking at how each model aggregates information, it's worth checking whether they end up encoding the sentence **the same way internally**: for the same short review, we extract every layer's **per-head self-attention matrices**, for both models, so individual heads can be compared side by side.

**Reading an attention heatmap**: each cell `(i, j)` shows how much token `i` attends to token `j` when building its contextual representation (rows sum to 1). A **strong diagonal** means tokens mostly look at themselves and their immediate neighbours (local, syntax-like processing); **vertical stripes** : a column lighting up across many rows mean many tokens converge onto a few "hub" tokens, often punctuation or the very first token, a well-documented **attention-sink** phenomenon; a **diffuse, low-contrast** matrix means attention is spread thinly across the whole sentence (more global, semantic mixing).

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-selfattn-example
#| fig-cap: "Self-attention matrices per head and per layer, for the same short review. One column per model; rows are grouped by layer (braced on the left), one head per row."

HAS_SA       = HAS_LA and all(SELF_ATTN.get(k) is not None for k in MODEL_KEYS)
HAS_PER_HEAD = HAS_SA and all("self_attn_per_head" in SELF_ATTN[k].files for k in MODEL_KEYS)

if not HAS_PER_HEAD:
    print("Per-head self-attention artifacts not yet available — re-run benchmark/explain.py with per-head export enabled.")
else:
    HAS_TOKENS = "tokens" in SELF_ATTN[MODEL_KEYS[0]].files

    # Pick a short example (15–22 tokens) so the per-head grid stays readable
    n_align  = min(len(SELF_ATTN[k]["texts"]) for k in MODEL_KEYS)
    lengths  = [SELF_ATTN[MODEL_KEYS[0]]["self_attn"][i].shape[-1] for i in range(n_align)]
    mid_idx  = [i for i, L in enumerate(lengths) if 15 <= L <= 22]
    lbl_idx  = mid_idx[0] if mid_idx else int(np.argmax(lengths))

    # self_attn_per_head[i] : (n_layers, n_heads, L, L)
    per_head    = {k: SELF_ATTN[k]["self_attn_per_head"][lbl_idx] for k in MODEL_KEYS}
    n_layers_ph = per_head[MODEL_KEYS[0]].shape[0]
    n_heads_ph  = per_head[MODEL_KEYS[0]].shape[1]
    L_ph        = per_head[MODEL_KEYS[0]].shape[-1]

    if HAS_TOKENS:
        tok_ph = list(SELF_ATTN[MODEL_KEYS[0]]["tokens"][lbl_idx])[:L_ph]
    else:
        raw_ph = str(SELF_ATTN[MODEL_KEYS[0]]["texts"][lbl_idx]).split()
        tok_ph = (raw_ph + [""] * L_ph)[:L_ph]

    n_cols = len(MODEL_KEYS)            # one column per model
    n_rows = n_layers_ph * n_heads_ph   # rows grouped by layer, one head per row

    vspace = 0.025
    row_h  = (1 - vspace * (n_rows - 1)) / n_rows

    def row_domain(r):  # r is 1-indexed, row 1 is at the top
        top = 1 - (r - 1) * (row_h + vspace)
        return top, top - row_h

    fig_ph = make_subplots(
        rows=n_rows, cols=n_cols,
        column_titles=[MODEL_LABELS[k] for k in MODEL_KEYS],
        horizontal_spacing=0.05, vertical_spacing=vspace,
    )

    for ri in range(n_rows):
        l, h  = divmod(ri, n_heads_ph)
        row    = ri + 1
        show_x = (row == n_rows)

        for ci, key in enumerate(MODEL_KEYS):
            col  = ci + 1
            idx  = (row - 1) * n_cols + col
            xref = "x" if idx == 1 else f"x{idx}"
            fig_ph.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
                z=per_head[key][l, h],
                x=tok_ph,
                y=tok_ph,
                coloraxis="coloraxis",
                hovertemplate="key=%{x}<br>query=%{y}<br>weight=%{z:.3f}"
                              + f"<extra>{MODEL_LABELS[key]} L{l + 1} H{h + 1}</extra>",
            ), row=row, col=col)
            fig_ph.update_xaxes(
                tickangle=-90, tickfont=dict(size=8),
                showticklabels=show_x,
                row=row, col=col,
            )
            fig_ph.update_yaxes(
                autorange="reversed", showticklabels=False,
                scaleanchor=xref, scaleratio=1,
                row=row, col=col,
            )

        # "Head N" label, just left of the plot
        top, bottom = row_domain(row)
        fig_ph.add_annotation(
            xref="paper", yref="paper",
            x=-0.04, y=(top + bottom) / 2,
            text=f"Head {h + 1}", showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10), xanchor="right", yanchor="middle",
        )

        # "Layer N" brace, spanning the n_heads_ph rows of this layer
        if h == n_heads_ph - 1:
            top1, _ = row_domain(ri - n_heads_ph + 2)
            _, bot2 = row_domain(row)
            x_tick, x_bar = -0.10, -0.13
            fig_ph.add_shape(
                type="path",
                path=f"M {x_tick},{top1} L {x_bar},{top1} L {x_bar},{bot2} L {x_tick},{bot2}",
                xref="paper", yref="paper",
                line=dict(color="black", width=1),
            )
            fig_ph.add_annotation(
                xref="paper", yref="paper",
                x=-0.18, y=(top1 + bot2) / 2,
                text=f"<b>Layer {l + 1}</b>", showarrow=False,
                font=dict(size=11), textangle=-90,
                xanchor="center", yanchor="middle",
            )

    cell_px = 16
    plot_sz = L_ph * cell_px

    snippet = str(SELF_ATTN[MODEL_KEYS[0]]["texts"][lbl_idx])
    fig_ph.update_layout(
        width=plot_sz * n_cols + 260,
        height=plot_sz * n_rows + 160,
        paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
        margin=dict(t=110, b=110, l=160, r=90),
        coloraxis=dict(colorscale="Viridis", cmin=0, colorbar=dict(title="weight", x=1.18, len=0.8)),
        title_text=f"Per-head self-attention — \"{snippet}\"",
        title_font_size=12,
    )
    fig_ph.show()
```

### Class representations: label queries vs. linear-head directions

At the very last step, both architectures reduce a review to a decision by comparing some representation of the sentence against **one reference vector per star rating**:

- Label Attention learns five **query embeddings** (`label_embeds`, one per class) that directly drive the cross-attention : they *are* the model's notion of "what a 1★ review looks like", "what a 5★ review looks like", and so on.
- Mean Pooling instead learns a plain **linear classification head**; each of its five weight rows (`linear_weight`) plays the same role : the direction in embedding space onto which the pooled sentence vector is projected for that class.

Despite coming from entirely different mechanisms, both sets of vectors answer the same question — "how does this architecture represent the five star ratings internally?" — so comparing their **geometry** is a fair, mechanism-agnostic probe.

**(a) Cosine similarity matrix** — how aligned are the five class vectors with one another, within each model? **Absolute cosine values are not comparable across the two models** (the two embedding spaces are unrelated and have different dimensionalities of meaning) — what matters is the *pattern*: are adjacent ratings (1★–2★, 4★–5★) closer to each other than distant ones (1★–5★)? A model whose class vectors trace out the **ordinal structure of star ratings** has, in some sense, "discovered" that this is a regression-like problem dressed up as classification.

**(b) 2D PCA projection** — a visual complement to (a): we project the five 256-dimensional vectors onto their first two principal components and connect them in rating order (1★→2★→…→5★). Again, **only the shape matters, not the absolute coordinates**: a smooth arc or line suggests an ordinal layout; a tight, undifferentiated cluster suggests the model leans more on *magnitude* (how far the sentence representation sits from a boundary) than on *direction* to separate ratings; an erratic zig-zag would be the most surprising — and most interesting — finding of all.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-class-vectors
#| fig-cap: "Class direction vectors compared across architectures. Top row: cosine similarity between the five per-class vectors (only the pattern is comparable across models, not the absolute values). Bottom row: their 2D PCA projection, connected in rating order 1★→5★ — a smooth arc indicates an ordinal internal layout."

def _cosine_sim(vecs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n = vecs / (np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True) + 1e-12)
    return n @ n.T

def _pca_2d(vecs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    centered = vecs - vecs.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)
    _, _, Vt = np.linalg.svd(centered, full_matrices=False)
    return centered @ Vt[:2].T

HAS_CV = HAS_LA and all(CLASS_VECS.get(k) is not None for k in MODEL_KEYS)

if not HAS_CV:
    print("Class-vector artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    titles = [f"{MODEL_LABELS[k]}<br><sub>({str(CLASS_VECS[k]['kind'])})</sub>" for k in MODEL_KEYS]
    fig_cv = make_subplots(
        rows=2, cols=len(MODEL_KEYS), subplot_titles=titles + [""] * len(MODEL_KEYS),
        vertical_spacing=0.16, row_heights=[0.45, 0.55],
    )
    for ci, key in enumerate(MODEL_KEYS):
        vecs = CLASS_VECS[key]["class_vectors"]

        sim = _cosine_sim(vecs)
        fig_cv.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
            z=sim, x=STARS, y=STARS, zmin=-1, zmax=1, colorscale="RdBu_r",
            texttemplate="%{z:.3f}", textfont=dict(size=10),
            showscale=(ci == len(MODEL_KEYS) - 1), colorbar=dict(title="cos", len=0.4, y=0.8),
        ), row=1, col=ci + 1)

        xy = _pca_2d(vecs)
        fig_cv.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=xy[:, 0], y=xy[:, 1], mode="markers+lines+text",
            text=STARS, textposition="top center", textfont=dict(size=11),
            marker=dict(size=13, color=PALETTE, line=dict(color="white", width=1)),
            line=dict(color="#bbb", dash="dot"), showlegend=False,
        ), row=2, col=ci + 1)

    fig_cv.update_yaxes(autorange="reversed", row=1)
    fig_cv.update_xaxes(title_text="PC1", row=2)
    fig_cv.update_yaxes(title_text="PC2", col=1, row=2)
    fig_cv.update_layout(height=720, paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
                         margin=dict(t=70, b=20, l=10, r=10))
    fig_cv.show()
```

### Inside label attention: does the mechanism agree with post-hoc explanations?

Label Attention is special: it doesn't *need* a post-hoc method like Captum to explain itself — its cross-attention weights (one query per class, attending over the sentence) are *already* a built-in, "by design" account of which words drove each class's score. This raises a natural question: does this **architectural** explanation agree with the **gradient-based** one? If it does, that's reassuring triangulation — two very different ways of probing the model converging on the same answer. If it doesn't, at least one of the two views is misleading, and it's worth knowing which.

(Mean Pooling has no such mechanism — by construction, this analysis only concerns the Label Attention model.)

**(a) Agreement with Captum.** For each review and each class, we compute the **Pearson correlation** between the (softmax-normalised) label-attention weights and the |Captum scores|, both at the word level. Values close to **+1** mean the two methods broadly agree on which words matter for that class; values near **0** mean they're unrelated; negative values (rare, but possible) would mean they actively disagree.

**(b) Head specialisation.** The label-attention module uses several heads per class. Are they redundant copies of each other, or does each develop its own point of view? For each head we compute how much its attention pattern *changes* depending on which class it serves: `1 − mean pairwise cosine similarity` between the head's five class-conditioned attention vectors. A score near **0** means the head looks at the same words regardless of the target class (a generic, class-agnostic feature detector); a score near **1** means the head reorganises its focus dramatically from one class to the next (a class-specialised head).

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-labatt-agreement
#| fig-cap: "Per-(review, class) Pearson correlation between Label Attention's own softmax weights and |Captum IG| scores, at the word level. Mass concentrated toward +1 means the architecture's built-in explanation agrees with the post-hoc gradient-based one."

HAS_LAT = HAS_LA and lat_la is not None

if not HAS_LAT:
    print("Label-attention artifacts not yet available (Mean Pooling has no label-attention mechanism).")
else:
    def _labatt_captum_agreement() -> np.ndarray:
        n = min(len(cap_la["words"]), len(lat_la["words"]))
        rhos = []
        for i in range(n):
            cap_mat = _class_sorted(cap_la, i)
            lat_mat = lat_la["word_attn"][i]
            nw = min(cap_mat.shape[1], lat_mat.shape[1])
            if nw < 3:
                continue
            for c in range(N_CLS):
                a = np.abs(cap_mat[c, :nw])
                b = lat_mat[c, :nw]
                if a.std() > 1e-9 and b.std() > 1e-9:
                    rhos.append(float(np.corrcoef(a, b)[0, 1]))
        return np.array(rhos)

    rhos = _labatt_captum_agreement()
    if len(rhos) == 0:
        print("Label-attention word weights are uniform (std ≈ 0) — artifact label_attn.npz needs to be regenerated via explain.py.")
    else:
        fig_agree = go.Figure(go.Histogram(x=rhos, nbinsx=30, marker_color=PALETTE[0], opacity=0.85))
        med = float(np.median(rhos))
        fig_agree.add_vline(x=med, line_dash="dash", line_color="#B71C1C",
                            annotation_text=f"median = {med:.2f}", annotation_position="top right")
        fig_agree.update_layout(
            xaxis_title="Pearson correlation (label-attention weights vs. |Captum IG|)",
            yaxis_title="Count over (review × class) pairs",
            paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white", height=420,
            margin=dict(t=30, b=20, l=10, r=10),
        )
        fig_agree.show()
```

The histogram above summarises agreement at the (review, class) level via a single correlation number. The two views below disaggregate this same comparison down to the **word level**: a scatter plot of every (Captum, label-attention) pair, and a side-by-side highlighted-text view for individual reviews.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-labatt-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Word-level scatter of Label Attention's softmax weight against |Captum IG|, one point per (review, class, word), faceted by star rating. r = Pearson correlation within each facet — the same pairs underlying the histogram above, shown disaggregated."

if not HAS_LAT:
    print("Label-attention artifacts not yet available (Mean Pooling has no label-attention mechanism).")
else:
    def _labatt_captum_points():
        n  = min(len(cap_la["words"]), len(lat_la["words"]))
        xs = [[] for _ in range(N_CLS)]
        ys = [[] for _ in range(N_CLS)]
        for i in range(n):
            cap_mat = _class_sorted(cap_la, i)
            lat_mat = lat_la["word_attn"][i]
            nw = min(cap_mat.shape[1], lat_mat.shape[1])
            if nw < 3:
                continue
            for c in range(N_CLS):
                xs[c].extend(np.abs(cap_mat[c, :nw]).tolist())
                ys[c].extend(lat_mat[c, :nw].tolist())
        return [np.array(x) for x in xs], [np.array(y) for y in ys]

    xs, ys = _labatt_captum_points()
    all_y  = np.concatenate(ys) if any(len(y) for y in ys) else np.array([])

    if all_y.size == 0 or all_y.std() < 1e-9:
        print("Label-attention word weights are uniform (std ≈ 0) — artifact label_attn.npz needs to be regenerated via explain.py.")
    else:
        fig_scatter = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=N_CLS, subplot_titles=STARS,
                                     shared_yaxes=True, horizontal_spacing=0.03)
        for c in range(N_CLS):
            x, y = xs[c], ys[c]
            if len(x) > 1 and x.std() > 1e-9 and y.std() > 1e-9:
                r = float(np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1])
            else:
                r = float("nan")

            fig_scatter.add_trace(go.Scattergl(
                x=x, y=y, mode="markers",
                marker=dict(size=3, color=PALETTE[c], opacity=0.12),
                showlegend=False,
            ), row=1, col=c + 1)

            ref_suffix = "" if c == 0 else str(c + 1)
            fig_scatter.add_annotation(
                text=f"r = {r:.2f}" if not np.isnan(r) else "r = n/a",
                xref=f"x{ref_suffix} domain", yref=f"y{ref_suffix} domain",
                x=0.95, y=0.97, showarrow=False,
                font=dict(size=12, color="#333"), bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)",
            )
            fig_scatter.update_xaxes(row=1, col=c + 1,
                                      title_text="|Captum IG|" if c == N_CLS // 2 else None)

        fig_scatter.update_yaxes(title_text="Label-attention weight", row=1, col=1)
        fig_scatter.update_layout(
            height=340, paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
            margin=dict(t=40, b=40, l=10, r=10),
        )
        fig_scatter.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-labatt-highlight
#| fig-cap: "Side-by-side highlighted-text view for the Label Attention model: the top row shades each word by its |Captum IG| score (green = for / red = against the selected class), the bottom row shades the same words by the architecture's own label-attention weight (blue intensity = share of attention received). Use the star buttons to switch class."

if not HAS_LAT:
    print("Label-attention artifacts not yet available (Mean Pooling has no label-attention mechanism).")
else:
    _sample = np.concatenate([lat_la["word_attn"][i].ravel()
                               for i in range(min(len(lat_la["word_attn"]), 100))])
    if _sample.std() < 1e-9:
        print("Label-attention word weights are uniform (std ≈ 0) — artifact label_attn.npz needs to be regenerated via explain.py.")
    else:
        y_true_lat = cap_la["y_true"].astype(int)
        y_pred_lat = cap_la["y_pred"].astype(int)
        rng2 = np.random.default_rng(7)

        n_pairs   = min(len(cap_la["words"]), len(lat_la["words"]))
        ok_lat    = np.where(y_pred_lat[:n_pairs] == y_true_lat[:n_pairs])[0]
        rng2.shuffle(ok_lat)
        picks_lat = ok_lat[:5].tolist()

        examples_lat = []
        for i in picks_lat:
            i = int(i)
            cap_mat = _class_sorted(cap_la, i)
            lat_mat = lat_la["word_attn"][i]
            nw = min(cap_mat.shape[1], lat_mat.shape[1], 40)
            words = [str(w) for w in list(cap_la["words"][i])[:nw]]
            examples_lat.append({
                "y_true": int(cap_la["y_true"][i]),
                "pred":   int(cap_la["y_pred"][i]),
                "text":   str(cap_la["texts"][i])[:200],
                "words":  words,
                "captum_scores": [cap_mat[c][:nw].tolist() for c in range(N_CLS)],
                "labatt_scores": [lat_mat[c][:nw].tolist() for c in range(N_CLS)],
            })

        hl2_html = f"""
<style>
  .hl2-wrap   {{ font-family:sans-serif; font-size:14px; }}
  .hl2-nav    {{ display:flex; align-items:center; gap:12px; justify-content:center; margin-bottom:12px; }}
  .hl2-btn    {{ padding:6px 18px; background:#f0f0f0; border:1px solid #ccc;
                 border-radius:5px; cursor:pointer; font-size:13px; }}
  .hl2-btn:hover {{ background:#e0e0e0; }}
  .hl2-count  {{ font-size:12px; color:#999; }}
  .hl2-card   {{ border:1px solid #e2e2e2; border-radius:8px; padding:14px 16px; background:#fcfcfc; }}
  .hl2-meta   {{ font-size:13px; color:#444; margin-bottom:10px; }}
  .hl2-row    {{ margin:6px 0 14px 0; }}
  .hl2-rlbl   {{ font-size:12.5px; color:#555; margin-bottom:4px; }}
  .hl2-words  {{ line-height:2.5; }}
  .hl2-cls-btn {{ padding:2px 9px; font-size:11px; border-radius:12px; cursor:pointer;
                  border:1.5px solid #555; margin:0 2px; transition:all .15s; font-weight:600; }}
</style>

<div class="hl2-wrap">
  <div class="hl2-nav">
    <button class="hl2-btn" onclick="hl2Nav(-1)">← Prev</button>
    <span class="hl2-count" id="hl2-count"></span>
    <button class="hl2-btn" onclick="hl2Nav(1)">Next →</button>
  </div>
  <div class="hl2-card">
    <div class="hl2-meta" id="hl2-meta"></div>
    <div id="hl2-rows"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {{
  const EX = {json.dumps(examples_lat)};
  const ST = {json.dumps(STARS)};
  let cur = 0;
  let selC = {{}};

  function esc(s) {{
    return String(s).replace(/&/g,'&amp;').replace(/</g,'&lt;').replace(/>/g,'&gt;');
  }}

  function spansSigned(words, scores) {{
    const amax = Math.max.apply(null, scores.map(function(x){{ return Math.abs(x); }})) || 1;
    return words.map(function(w, j) {{
      const s     = j < scores.length ? scores[j] : 0;
      const t     = Math.abs(s) / amax;
      const rgb   = s >= 0 ? '57,255,20' : '255,30,30';
      const alpha = (0.10 + 0.65 * t).toFixed(2);
      return '<span style="background-color:rgba(' + rgb + ',' + alpha + ');'
           + 'padding:1px 3px;margin:0 1px;line-height:2.3;">'
           + esc(w) + '</span>';
    }}).join(' ');
  }}

  function spansUnsigned(words, scores) {{
    const amax = Math.max.apply(null, scores.map(function(x){{ return Math.abs(x); }})) || 1;
    return words.map(function(w, j) {{
      const s     = j < scores.length ? scores[j] : 0;
      const t     = Math.abs(s) / amax;
      const alpha = (0.10 + 0.65 * t).toFixed(2);
      return '<span style="background-color:rgba(21,101,192,' + alpha + ');'
           + 'padding:1px 3px;margin:0 1px;line-height:2.3;">'
           + esc(w) + '</span>';
    }}).join(' ');
  }}

  function render(i) {{
    const e  = EX[i];
    document.getElementById('hl2-count').textContent = (i + 1) + ' / ' + EX.length;
    const ok = e.pred === e.y_true;
    const bc = ok ? '#2E7D32' : '#B71C1C';
    document.getElementById('hl2-meta').innerHTML =
      '<b>Review #' + i + '</b> &mdash; true rating: '
      + '<span style="color:#222;font-weight:700;">' + ST[e.y_true] + '</span>'
      + ' &mdash; predicted: <span style="color:' + bc + ';font-weight:700;">' + ST[e.pred] + '</span>'
      + ' &mdash; <i>&ldquo;' + esc(e.text).slice(0, 170) + '&hellip;&rdquo;</i>';

    const c     = selC[i] !== undefined ? selC[i] : e.pred;
    const btns  = ST.map(function(star, k) {{
      const active = k === c;
      const isPred = k === e.pred;
      const col    = isPred ? bc : '#333';
      return '<button class="hl2-cls-btn" onclick="hl2SelClass(' + i + ',' + k + ')"'
           + ' style="border-color:' + col + ';color:' + (active ? '#fff' : col) + ';background:' + (active ? col : '#fff') + ';">'
           + star + '</button>';
    }}).join('');

    let h = '<div style="margin-bottom:8px;"><span style="font-size:11px;color:#888;">attributions for:</span> ' + btns + '</div>';
    h += '<div class="hl2-row">'
       + '<div class="hl2-rlbl"><b>Captum IG</b> <span style="font-size:11px;color:#888;">(green = for / red = against, gradient-based)</span></div>'
       + '<div class="hl2-words">' + spansSigned(e.words, e.captum_scores[c]) + '</div>'
       + '</div>';
    h += '<div class="hl2-row">'
       + '<div class="hl2-rlbl"><b>Label Attention</b> <span style="font-size:11px;color:#888;">(blue = attention received, architecture-native)</span></div>'
       + '<div class="hl2-words">' + spansUnsigned(e.words, e.labatt_scores[c]) + '</div>'
       + '</div>';
    document.getElementById('hl2-rows').innerHTML = h;
  }}

  window.hl2SelClass = function(exIdx, classIdx) {{
    selC[exIdx] = classIdx;
    render(cur);
  }};
  window.hl2Nav = function(d) {{ cur = (cur + d + EX.length) % EX.length; render(cur); }};
  render(0);
}})();
</script>
"""
        display(HTML(hl2_html))
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-labatt-heads
#| fig-cap: "Head specialisation in the Label Attention module: for each head, 1 − mean pairwise cosine similarity between its five class-conditioned (token-level) attention vectors, averaged over reviews (error bars = ±1 std). Higher = the head reshapes its focus more from one star rating to the next."

if not HAS_LAT:
    print("Label-attention artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    def _head_specialisation(lat) -> np.ndarray:
        n       = len(lat["head_attn"])
        n_heads = lat["head_attn"][0].shape[0]
        iu      = np.triu_indices(N_CLS, k=1)
        spec    = np.zeros((n, n_heads))
        for i in range(n):
            a  = lat["head_attn"][i]                                  # (n_heads, n_classes, seq_len)
            if a.shape[-1] == 0:
                continue
            an = a / (np.linalg.norm(a, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-12)
            for h in range(n_heads):
                sim = an[h] @ an[h].T
                vals = sim[iu]
                spec[i, h] = 1.0 - vals.mean() if len(vals) > 0 else 0.0
        return spec

    spec    = _head_specialisation(lat_la)
    n_heads = spec.shape[1]
    if n_heads == 0 or spec.shape[0] == 0:
        print("Head specialisation data unavailable.")
    else:
        fig_heads = go.Figure(go.Bar(
            x=[f"Head {h + 1}" for h in range(n_heads)],
            y=spec.mean(axis=0),
            error_y=dict(type="data", array=spec.std(axis=0), visible=True),
            marker_color=[PALETTE[h % len(PALETTE)] for h in range(n_heads)],
        ))
        fig_heads.update_layout(
            yaxis_title="Class-specialisation score  (0 = generic head, 1 = fully class-specific)",
            yaxis_range=[0, 1.0], paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
            height=400, margin=dict(t=30, b=20, l=10, r=10),
        )
        fig_heads.show()
```

Together, these two views tell a coherent story about *how much you can trust the architecture's self-reported reasoning*: strong agreement with Captum (a) combined with clearly differentiated heads (b) would mean the label-attention weights are a faithful, fine-grained, "for free" explanation — arguably Label Attention's strongest practical advantage over Mean Pooling, which offers no such built-in introspection.

### Are these explanations faithful? A word-deletion stress test

All the analyses so far take attribution scores at face value but a high attribution score is only meaningful if the model would *actually* change its mind were that word removed. This is the **faithfulness** question, and the standard way to test it the "comprehensiveness" test from the ERASER benchmark : progressively delete the words Captum ranked as most important for the predicted class, and watch how fast that class's predicted probability collapses. As a control, we also delete the *same number* of **randomly chosen** words, averaged over 3 draws, isolating the effect of *which* words are removed from the trivial effect of mutilating the sentence at all.

How to read it:

- The **solid lines** (guided removal) should drop *faster* than the **dotted lines** (random removal) of the same colour : the larger that gap, the more the explanation reflects words the model genuinely depends on, rather than scores that merely *correlate* with importance without *causing* it.
- If a model's guided and random curves are nearly **superimposed**, its Captum attributions are not very faithful: deleting the words it called "important" hurts the prediction no more than deleting random ones  the explanation is, in a meaningful sense, decorative.
- Comparing the **gap** between the two curves across architectures tells you which one's attributions are more trustworthy *as a debugging/auditing tool* — a property that matters in production independently of raw accuracy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-explain-faithfulness
#| fig-cap: "Faithfulness (comprehensiveness) test: mean probability retained by the originally-predicted class as the most-attributed words are progressively deleted (solid = guided removal by |Captum score|; dotted = random-removal control, averaged over 3 draws). Shaded bands show ±1 std across 50 reviews."

HAS_FAI = HAS_LA and all(FAITHFUL.get(k) is not None for k in MODEL_KEYS)

if not HAS_FAI:
    print("Faithfulness artifacts not yet available.")
else:
    fig_fai = go.Figure()
    for key in MODEL_KEYS:
        fai   = FAITHFUL[key]
        fr    = fai["fractions"]
        color = PALETTE[0] if key == "labatt" else PALETTE[4]
        rgb   = "21,101,192" if key == "labatt" else "183,28,28"
        for arr, name, dash in [(fai["guided_probs"], "guided removal", "solid"),
                                (fai["random_probs"], "random removal (control)", "dot")]:
            m, s = np.nanmean(arr, axis=0), np.nanstd(arr, axis=0)
            fig_fai.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                x=np.concatenate([fr, fr[::-1]]), y=np.concatenate([m + s, (m - s)[::-1]]),
                fill="toself", fillcolor=f"rgba({rgb},0.10)", line=dict(width=0),
                showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip",
            ))
            fig_fai.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                x=fr, y=m, mode="lines+markers", name=f"{MODEL_LABELS[key]} — {name}",
                line=dict(color=color, dash=dash, width=2.5), marker=dict(size=6),
            ))
    fig_fai.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="Fraction of words removed", yaxis_title="Mean retained probability of the predicted class",
        yaxis_range=[0, 1.02], paper_bgcolor="white", plot_bgcolor="white",
        height=460, margin=dict(t=30, b=20, l=10, r=10),
        legend=dict(x=1.02, y=1, xanchor="left"),
    )
    fig_fai.show()

    print("Faithfulness gap — area between the random and guided curves (higher = more faithful):\n")
    for key in MODEL_KEYS:
        fai = FAITHFUL[key]
        gap = float(np.trapezoid(np.nanmean(fai["random_probs"], 0) - np.nanmean(fai["guided_probs"], 0), fai["fractions"]))
        print(f"  {MODEL_LABELS[key]:<16} {gap:+.4f}")
```
